text: Thank you.
So I'm talking about two years of Skywatch Blue, and I'm going to try and talk a little
bit.
The mic isn't picking up your voice, you said it had something.
Oh, it fell off.
Thank you.
Let's just try clipping it to my shirt.
All right.
How's that?
Thank you.
All right.
So I'm talking about two years of running Skywatch Blue.
And I'm going to try and impart a few lessons learned for community labelers.
And some of these are hard-won lessons for, you know, really anyone who's looking to maybe
start a labeler, but doesn't have a ton of experience both with the tech, but also I think
with sort of traditional trust and safety.
So just by way of introduction, as Nick introduced me, I'm Julia.
You also may know me as Scoyatolo, and I am not sure why that slide didn't advance.
There we go.
And I have been for the last two years running Skywatch Blue.
Now, I'm going to preface this by saying that this isn't exactly the talk that I set out
to just, you know, share, you know, when I proposed this talk.
After this weekend, you know, talking to folks, I've been constantly tweaking the presentation.
And if I had time following Dr. K, I probably would have made some changes because I learned
some things during that talk that apply to some of the things that I'm going to say.
So, what are labels?
Labels are metadata.
And this is the sort of actual definition provided by the AT proto docs.
And it's kind of, it's a long explanation.
But the two things sort of to keep in mind about them is that they're a form of metadata about
any account or content in the AT proto ecosystem.
They sort of exist as freestanding objects.
And they can be used for moderation purposes.
But they can also be used for other purposes.
And there's quite a few labelers out there that are sort of novelty or fun labelers.
All right.
So I have an issue here where the slides don't exactly advance with sort of part that has the
notes.
So if it takes me a moment to move between slides, I apologize.
So what is Skywatch?
It is a general purpose labeling service.
It's one of the largest, well, it might be one of the largest community labelers on BlueSky.
There's no actual way to measure that other than by who has sort of clicked the heart button
on the labeler.
Which doesn't subscribe you to the labeler, by the way.
It's possibly a cautionary tale.
And it is definitely a personal project that got out of hand.
Hence the picture of Fred Thompson in A Hunt for Red October where he's doing this business
will get out of hand, it will get out of hand, and we'll be lucky to live through it.
I would also like to note, and I will come back to this in a couple of slides, Skywatch is
an inherently political project.
Not exactly in the way you think, but in the sense that all labelers are political.
So what is Skywatch not?
Well, Skywatch is not a community.
And this is worth noting, it is a community service, but one of the sort of key things to remember
about Skywatch is that it started with a few dozen users in mind.
And it grew at a slow rate until November of 2024, paralleling sites growth, at which point
it exploded by an order of magnitude.
I guess to that end, it's also hard to say that it serves a specific community.
And one thing I haven't cracked is what to do about that.
If you start with an original community of a few dozen, and now you are up at potentially
8,000 or more users who come from maybe backgrounds that you didn't originally plan for, it's hard
to say how to serve them best.
I also believe that Skywatch isn't a moderation service.
We're not doing traditional trust and safety.
We're also not -- we don't have takedown capabilities.
Labelers are metadata.
You can have block lists associated with certain labels, which we do, but block lists are soft
moderation in the sense that they don't necessarily affect -- they don't affect takedowns.
There was a recent paper actually published, which talked about the fact that they don't
really break up the social graph either.
And that's probably a good thing, even though the block on blue sky is understood to be a
nuclear block.
I will say that most labelers are not hard moderation.
Some exceptions would be, you know, black sky, north sky, and of course, blue sky moderation.
So then -- by way of example, I just wanted to show some of the labels that we sort of append
to content.
I've broken them out by account versus post.
And when I say that we're appending metadata, that's exactly what we're doing.
We're analyzing the content in one way or another, some of it via automation, some of it via manual
review, and then we are applying a label based on the rules and the definitions of the label
that we provide.
So I do want to, before I go on further, go back to the notion that Skywatch is an inherently
political project.
And to do that, I want to talk a little bit about why I started it.
In 2018, I co-authored a paper with Kate Starbird and her team at the University of Washington
that looked at the media ecosystem around the Syrian civil defense, also known as the White
Helmets, in Syria during that country's civil war.
And we started with Twitter.
Some of her team went on to analyze other platforms.
And what we found was that there was an active attempt to reshape the information space through
content sharing and remixing across multiple sites, domains, and political leanings to create
the appearance of independent confirmation.
The goal was to contest the civilian status of the White Helmets and legitimize their targeting
under international law.
Skywatch came about because Blue Sky offered a means to shape my personal information space.
And the information space of anybody who chose to use the labeler, which at the time was
a small handful, well, a few dozen people.
And I think it's important to remember that all labelers and content moderation do this to
some extent.
And whether the admins of it are conscious of it or not, we should be honest about that.
And so I've always tried to be open about my motivations, which is that Skywatch is opinionated.
It embodies a set of values and ideas about what users want to see on their timelines.
And so this is what I mean when I say the project is inherently political.
However, there are a couple other reasons why I started it.
Jerry Nerd sniped me.
So the first label was really the Blue Sky Elder label, which follows basically the criteria
laid out here.
And then next is, and content warning, but my partner who's in the room created this monstrosity,
and it became part of early Blue Sky culture, which is why we have a Sensual ALF label.
Yes, there are community standards, and I am trying to live up to them here.
All right.
So you want to start a label, a labeler, and a non-novelty labeler at that.
I think the first thing I'll say is, don't.
If you do, you're probably someone who enjoys getting yelled at.
You're now a mod, whether you like it or not, even though I said we're not a moderation service.
Every label is going to be perceived as a personal attack.
I mentioned you're getting yelled at.
Which to be fair to users, we don't have, if you're not hosting a PDS and having a labeler
associated with the PDS, you have no way to do the two-way communication.
You can get appeals in, but you can't explain to people why they were labeled in a private
manner.
There's no way to send them an email, and that's a shortfall of the tooling.
Folks don't read the label definitions, except for the rules layers, and they're always going
to be trying to sort of work you to get the rules, sort of either talk you into removing
a label from them or to apply a label to someone else because they think it fits your definition.
And I think a lot of folks, this is getting a little bit better, but I think a lot of folks
don't understand what community labels are and can't necessarily tell the difference between
you and blue sky.
So, one thing you're going to have to learn is that, I'm not sure, I'm sorry, your signals
are noisy.
And there we go.
Many of your user-generated signals are noisy.
You are, to a subset of users, going to be a gun and they are going to attempt to weaponize
you.
But, as I said before, lots of folks don't necessarily read the labels and, as such, they're going
to submit a lot of reports that have nothing to do with the things you label, per se.
Case in point, we are constantly getting reports of things that people suspect are AI.
Now, they are not necessarily misrepresenting what they are.
I discovered this morning, people had started reporting Addy.
It's just a fact of life.
And user reports are noisy.
Automation can fix some of this, but automation is also capable of making mistakes at scale.
So there's always going to be risks inherent in either approach.
Now in conversation with Luca and Juliette earlier this weekend, one thing that did come
up is that there are ways to mitigate this through tweaks to the reporting workflow.
So Brian, I'm sorry, you're probably going to get some GitHub issues from either me or Luca,
requesting changes when you all have the time.
And this is difficult if you don't control the reporting workflow.
Scale is very hard.
And if we go back to the fact that, yeah, I know, I went fully insane and I think the
website probably, you know, was going through some things.
Although interestingly, well, I'll get to it in a minute, but remember, so growth can be
a bit of a curse, especially with a general purpose labeler.
You know, one of the things that makes this difficult is not having a specific community
that you serve makes it a little bit harder to find and onboard volunteers.
So it's something to keep in mind.
So we had an eternal November that, well, not, not eternal.
It lasted about five months.
We were getting, so November was 70,000 reports, November 2024.
And interestingly, I was talking to Brian and this was actually different for them because,
because we don't deal with things like sexual content.
We didn't have to deal with the flood of reports that came in when video was rolled out.
So it's essentially unique to each labeler and kind of what your labels are and what people
perceive that you are covering.
Reports began climbing, you know, right after election week or election day.
They peaked in November 20th or so.
And then there was a steady decline over the next four to five months.
One thing I think that helped was that Blue Sky made some changes to the reporting workflow
so that you could no longer sort of shotgun report content to every single labeler you subscribe
to.
It forced you to be a little bit more nuanced.
And we've now settled out at a baseline of between three to 7,000 reports a month.
It varies.
And I suspect that as we get closer to midterms, we'll probably see a spike there as well as
people start reporting certain content to Skywatch.
So the other thing to keep in mind is that you're going to create new affordances.
And some of them are going to suck.
And you should undo your mistake.
So this is actually not a screenshot of Skywatch.
This labeler would put a badge on your account if you followed one of these individuals with
the theory of change being that by making this legible, which is what labels do, they make
certain things and behaviors legible.
It would cause users to shun certain accounts and some social pressure for folks to unfollow
those people.
Unfortunately, it facilitated abuse, which is why it no longer exists.
So you're going to make mistakes.
Some of them are going to be innocuous.
Slurs, for example, are hard to do, to sort of automatically flag.
You'll decide one day that you want to label an unfortunately far too common English language
slur, ableist slur, go to bed and you're going to wake up with the francophone world mad at
you because you forgot that it's a benign vocabulary word in French.
And so you need to approach these things with some nuance and fortunately we have the tooling to do.
So, though imperfect as it is, we do have the tooling to determine what language a given post
is in.
So you're going to want to label behavior and facts, not identity.
And you're going to want to do it consistently with your label definitions.
I think it should go without saying, but don't libel people.
Document your claims.
Be ethical.
You probably want to check your renter's or homeowner's insurance to make sure that, if
you're not a business this is, to make sure that you have just a sort of universal umbrella
coverage in case somebody decides to hit you with a slap lawsuit.
You are generally covered, at least from the conversations I've had with lawyers, you are
generally covered under section 230, if -- Or not.
Or not.
Don't build up to it.
Yes.
Yes.
Thank Ron Wyden for fighting a good fight.
But, you know, your mileage may vary by jurisdiction, of course, and, you know, there's absolutely
no certainty, you know, in a climate where speech is generally highly politicized.
But, labelers can be fun, and I, you know, don't want to just talk about the negative parts
of it.
And so, there are affordances of labelers, and this is not really in Skywatch's remit,
but I was testing whether or not I could use Osprey's database to figure out who was the
top Taylor Swift listener that day using, you know, using the Clickhouse database, and using
teal.fm's lexicon.
And I could.
And it was pretty simple.
And, you know, it's kind of funny, because then you can kind of go from there and independently
invent something very similar to Addy, where you take the posts of the top teal.fm listeners
of Taylor Swift tracks, and you just find some of their posts, and now you have a feed that
you could potentially create.
Again, not really within the scope of what we do, but I do want to sort of point out that
that labelers can be fun.
So looking a bit to the future, I want to talk a bit about some of the tools available
to make this a little bit easier.
So if you follow me on Blue Sky, you've probably seen a graph similar to this.
This is a graph of the sort of the Gaza Spam accounts that have cropped up in the last two
weeks.
The accounts where they are recycling the same set of usernames or the same set of profiles,
and doing things like reposting people so it just shows up in their mentions.
It is generally a good indicator of inauthentic behavior, and we're labeling it as inauthenticity.
And I know that this specific case is controversial, but this is more by way of example.
To do this, I would never have been able to do this or detect this at scale, just using
ozone and manually labeling things.
So we can make it suck less by using better tools.
So our automod started as a bash script, this was two years ago.
That lasted about a year, I rewrote it in TypeScript, and now we use Osprey.
And it's great.
We also use, it's Osprey plus ozone.
And then I use Claude and a stack of skills and tools to help me rapidly query the database.
So Claude is not making, now you see here Claude has opinions, but Claude is not making judgments
or content moderation decisions.
I've actually experimented in the past with trying to use LLMs to assess individual pieces
of content, and there's just not enough information there, at least for the tools I was using.
Now, there are probably more sophisticated tools, there's fine tuning you can probably do to
get your accuracy up.
But for the most part, what I find it extremely useful for is going out, querying Ozone, querying
the ClickHouse database to put together reports that I can then dive deeper into to see is this
really a signal or is this just something spurious?
And in this case, I have, to illustrate it, you know, there's basically a small network
of accounts via, you know, coming via Bridgifed that are sort of reposting what you would consider,
you know, Russian military telegram copy pasta and sort of other, you can call it propaganda,
other content aimed at various different language groups.
It's a relatively small number, but it's there.
And, you know, if you have these tools, it's relatively easy to find.
You know, and to that end, I've been building some of those tools such as sidecar services
to do, you know, Poisson analysis on PDS sign ups, you know, looking at URL over dispersion,
doing some lighting clustering and account entropy calculations to help detect that activity.
I'll say that those are noisy signals.
So, by themselves, they shouldn't be sort of relied upon.
So, human review is still important and correlation is still important between sources, between signals.
And I just want to end on the note that automation is not perfect, it can make mistakes, but it's a lot easier
than trying to find these things just using regular expressions or just using, you know, searches on Blue Sky.
And since our users subscribe for, especially for some of the sort of, you know, authenticity labels,
it's important for us to kind of proactively search for these things.
So, what is the future of unintegrated labelers?
And by unintegrated labelers, I mean what Skywatch is.
We are not affiliated with a set of PDSs or an app view in the way that Blue Sky is or that Black Sky is.
Now, my theory is that, because the atmosphere is growing, that not every application or PDS admin
or relay owner is going to want to set up their own full-stack moderation service.
They are going to need trusted signals to action content and accounts on their network.
A great example, excuse me, is PDS admins.
And I want to give a shout-out to Bailey for building Label Watcher.
If you haven't talked to Bailey about it, this is a tool for a PDS admin or any other piece of infrastructure to watch the firehose coming off of a set of labelers.
And then it can either email you to tell you when a label of interest is detected being admitted against an account on your PDS or it can automate the takedown.
And the nice thing about that, it is up to you as the admin to make a decision whether you trust the labeler enough to actually do the takedown.
And so as a result, we have this new group that previously wasn't considered a user that is now a user.
And so Skywatch is one of the signals for catching spam accounts created on self-hosted social and PMX social.
So thank you, Bailey, for building this and also for placing your trust in Skywatch.
I think we nerd sniped ourselves with that one.
So now I do just want to talk about this a little bit because the previous slide we talked a little bit about how Skywatch can be used by PDS admins.
And I want to note that labelers are an informal governance institution in a sense.
In a commons that, you know, as it grows, it's going to lack formal governance over the whole commons.
Blue sky is going to take responsibility for certain parts of it.
Other parts of it, other folks will.
And other parts of it may be somewhat ungoverned.
We should remember that labelers are embodying sort of a form of power and authority.
They're agents which act on the network.
And we should also note that some of these tools are incredibly powerful surveillance mechanisms.
And so they need to be used ethically.
We need moderation.
But that, by its nature, is surveillance.
And so seeing like a moderator means trying to take behavior and activity and making it legible.
And that creates the temptation to fence in the commons, categorize people in behavior in a binary fashion, and then tightly define what's right and wrong, as Dr. K sort of had alluded to.
Or, sorry, no, that was a previous session in here that was Dr. Blackwell.
We don't want to fence in the commons entirely.
We want to be able to sort of set norms and set the rules to govern the parts of the commons
that we care about that are within our jurisdiction without trying to restrict how everyone else
can act.
All right, I am very close to wrapping up then, so thank you.
I do want to talk about the elephant in the dark forest, because I'm an independent labeler.
If you've not read or watched the three-body problem, the dark forest metaphor is the idea
that all advanced galactic civilizations are locked in a bit of real-life game theory, which
the annihilation of any other civilization that they encounter is the only correct move.
And this is kind of a tortured metaphor, because I was going for the elephant in the room plus
the dark forest, and I don't necessarily want to cast dispersions on elephants.
But I think we do need to talk a little bit about Aegis.
If you don't know what Aegis was, it was one of the first independent labelers.
The operator ended up using it for harassment, and I think it soured Blue Sky, both the community
and the company, on the community labeler experiment for a while.
And I want to remind people that I, as an individual, sometimes with help, sometimes with not, am running
something that has the capability of being a surveillance platform.
Labelers collapse context, and at scale, they could potentially break the social graph.
And I think it's great that people put their trust in me.
But by the same token, you shouldn't have to.
You shouldn't have to trust that Fane will take away the keys if I suddenly go rogue.
And you know, it's a bad model.
In a previous life, I worked on issues of data ethics and privacy, and the harms technology
can cause inhumanitarian response.
Jay, quoting Melvin Kranzberg yesterday, I think was important.
These tools are what we make of them.
But we can't assume we're the only actors using them.
And the assumption that we are acting in an ethical and just way, I want to think that I am.
I want other people to think that I am.
But I don't want you to have to rely on just that.
And I think that we probably have to have some conversations as a sort of a community about
how we govern the commons and how we govern labelers.
So I would like to encourage people to come to the discourse and start discussions there
and move us towards a composable moderation working group so we can set some standards and
have some of these discussions.
And I just want to wrap up with this quote from Lawrence, "An open protocol that does
not answer the governance question will still receive an answer, yet it will be written by
whichever actors make themselves indispensable first."
And then I do want to finish on the note that I do think labelers are important.
I think that they're incredibly useful.
I think we have the opportunity to shape the spaces that we are sort of inhabiting for good.
And so even though this talk has had pessimistic notes, I want to end on the note that you should
check out some of these labelers.
Skywatch is there, of course, but Lylabs, SteshLab, and Black Sky are also labelers that I use.
I find valuable, and I hope you do too.
And I think we are probably just at time, so I don't want to run over.
But if there are any questions, I'm happy to hang out for a little bit and answer them.
Thank you.
